speaker: interviewer
text: Tape two. Santa, you are now in Spain. Can you describe your life in Spain and what happened to you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There is just one important thing I have to say about my father. When he was on the train from Paris to go to San Sebastian, I think it was-- yes, San Sebastian-- the border between France and Spain, in the same compartment as he and my brother, Levi, was a gentleman. He happened to be a Russian Jew who had settled in Spain and married a Catholic woman. He had children. Of course they were not Jewish. They were Catholic. And my father spoke to him and they discussed. And he says, don't worry, I will help you. When we come there, you come where I live, and I will help you find something. And sure enough, he was honest. He meant it the right way, only it wasn't the right way for us-- for us children, because it was out of the way. My father should have carried on. He should have gone to a town, to Barcelona. He should of thought-- by then I was 15, it's true.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I came only a year later when I was 16. He should have thought that a 16-year-old girl should have something, a company or what. No. Nobody blamed him actually because he wasn't himself. But because this gentleman, this Russian Jew helped him, it helped him, you see. And they arrived in Bilbao eventually, from San Sebastian to Bilbao, and from Bilbao they took a train and they went to a place called by Baracaldo. And this is where my father settled. He-- you want me to spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, thank you. B-A-R-A-C-A-L-D-O, Baracaldo. But I don't know, also about 20 kilometers from Bilbao. I'm not sure but some distance away from Bilbao, the main town. And there my father settled way out near the Pyrenees. We could see the Pyrenees. It was beautiful. But he said they bought a dilapidated shack. Three rooms were there, and there he settled. And for the 1,000-- he paid from the 1,000 marks for that, and also he paid for an incubator. All of a sudden he became a breeder. He put in-- I don't know if it helped-- 200 eggs, I believe it held. And out of that came three chicks-- three or four chicks. And they died eventually also. Poor man, really. He had no luck whatsoever. He was completely subdued. He was completely devastated, what had happened to him from the Nazi persecution. My brother Leib immediately went to school, and he did very, very well. And when my mother arrived three months later, when she saw what was happening, she didn't know what to do. She would not touch her 1,000 marks. And eventually the money for my father just ran out. So she decided to take one of her suitcases, go to town with my father who spoke Spanish by then, and buy linen-- all sorts of linen. She took this suitcase with the linen-- you know, linen is very heavy-- from place to place, which at first it was very difficult to infiltrate to houses because they had these little peepholes, which we didn't have in Germany then. You didn't need them. There was honesty. And in Spain, apparently people are not so honest. They locked themselves in. We have got this, of course, today here. And it took a long time until she established a clientele. But afterwards, I don't know how long it took her, they welcomed her. Oh, Maria is here. Yes, I want to say that we all changed our names. We didn't change the surname, but we changed. My mother, Maita, she became Maria. My father became Simon, which is a very popular-- Simone-- in France. My brother Levi became Leon, very popular name. There is a province called Leon. And Chaim, very easy. There must have been lots of Jews by the name of Chaim who converted and gave it to their children. They are called Jaime, but it's spelled with a J, because a J is a 'hu' in Spain, and they pronounced a 'hu' like the Jews, Jaime. So we were all-- and I was Santa. Very easy, it's a Spanish name. Why my father gave me the name Santa is still a mystery to me. Anyhow, the thing is that-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --they are there, and my mother was very much liked, but she worked worked hard. She worked very hard. And there was no [NON-ENGLISH]. There were at lagerheads, my father, my mother. Because my father just gave in. He did nothing. He just sat there. He was still young. He could have done something-- dig the garden, do something. And that aggravated my mother. He was a man full of enthusiasm in Germany. And he had nothing. And she wanted him to do something for his own sake. He refused. So there was no [NON-ENGLISH]. And then when I arrived a year later, and I saw my mother do this, coming home in the evening, exhausted, I was devastated. I didn't know what to do, and I couldn't help her. I said, I come along with you. And she says, no. No, that's not for you. And I became ill. I became very ill. And they diagnosed liver illness. Well, I didn't understand it was-- and it was the stomach. But it took a long time for me to rehabilitate myself. It took a year or two or more. I was quite ill. And I became thin, emaciated. But then, after a while, I blossomed out. Now that was in 1934, 1935, 1936. The Spanish war erupted. Franco-- the General Franco-- wanted to take over, make it seem like Germany now is strong with a national theme. And Russia, they didn't want Russia. And Italy was strong. He also wanted to be the strong man of Spain. It's true. Spain was in the doldrums. And he had an idea to build it up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So for that, he engaged lots of Moors, and he promised them, if you capture a town, you are free to loot and you're free to rape. And that, of course, frightened the inhabitants. Now, we lived in the North. And Franco started in the South. And the war carried on and on and on. It took 2 and 1/2 years actually. Now, '36-- we were not bombarded in '36, but already in '37 we were being bombarded. So there were no shelters. What do the Spanish people know about shelters? So we ran under the sewers. And there we hid. Or under the mattress, under the bed. That's where we hid. And of course things become scarce. And all of a sudden, my father woke up. He went to the farms, and he bought chickens and he bought things, and he sold them to the bakeries or he exchanged-- barter. We had plenty to eat all of a sudden. We were well to do. And I  even got a fur coat, if you don't mind. How this came about, I don't know, but I've got a fur coat from, all of a sudden, from my father. Now in 1937, I forgot which months-- I don't remember that very well-- the war had already come nearer, and we saw the flares. Oh, it's beautiful, but it's really very dangerous, these flares. And it became so bad that we were now under fire. And all the Spanish people started running and we too. We had to run. So where do you run? You run to the station. Everybody running towards the station. And the trains were very busy. And what did my parents take, at least my mother? She didn't take suitcases. She took huge sacks and stuffed them with the [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] are very important. It came from Poland which, her mother had given her, and which she now is taking along with her. [NON-ENGLISH] you don't leave behind. Dresses you can get eventually. Just explain what [NON-ENGLISH] are.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] is eiderdown.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eiderdown.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, eiderdown, with loose, beautiful goose feathers. Really beautiful. I have got them now, because that is what she gave me for my trousseau when I came to marry my husband. So I've got it from Poland. And she took a few necessities also. And we all schlepped with sacks now. We're all schlepping sacks. We come to the station. And at the station there is chaos. Chaos. People running, people shouting, people screaming. And you know, the Spanish people are very excitable. And I am very excitable too. We heard a train had been bombarded, and there won't be any train for some time. But we stood there and we waited. Eventually, hours later, a train did arrive. My father and my-- Leon did not get on that train. So they went separate. We didn't know where they went, but they went separate. Lots of people didn't get on. But my mother, myself, and Chain, we got onto the train. We were chock a block on the train. And after about-- traveling for about an hour, it stopped. Now, I have a vivid imagination, a very vivid imagination. We were at  an embankment. And people were telling how the Moors were allowed to rape the girls. And it was evening, actually. And in my imagination, all of a sudden I saw the Moors coming with the round knives, coming over. And I started hysterically saying, 'Moros, Moros.' And I shouted, 'Moros!' And there were no Moors.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my imagination-- and everybody in the train started being terribly shouting, screaming. No Moros! There are no Moros! What are you shouting? My mother gave me one, two, and I woke up. But I was terrified. I thought there were Moors. Well, the train started going again, no Moors. Thank God.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after a certain time, we arrived at the place. We couldn't go further because the lines had been bombarded. Off we went with the thousands of others. And then we came and followed. We just followed. We didn't know what to do so we followed. And we came onto a highway, everybody going with their suitcases and with their parcels and so on. And many lorries were going towards Baracaldo, towards this way, where we came from to bring ammunition to fight.

speaker: interviewer
text: But others came back empty. Do you think they would stop? People were shouting and screaming. No, they would not stop. They did not stop. Until about much later, one stopped. Chaim, he took the bags and threw them in, put my mother, threw her in. People were scrambling, and then he grabbed me, and the three of us we're on with-- I don't know how many others. And it took us to a certain place, like a small town. We got off and it was chaos. Refugees all over. And they were being accommodated all over. And there was no more place. So they accommodated them in a church. The church was full of people. So we clambered up the stairs. And as we clambered up the stairs, and you passed people and their parcels, there was a place, so we sat down. And that night we slept on this thing, wondering what is going to be? What is going to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the next morning, chaos again. Cars arriving and noises and so on. And we heard screaming, crying, people who had lost their-- heard that they lost their dear ones. We came down to the place there. It's a plaza. The church is always at the plaza with the school sets like that. The mother looking after the-- and Spain is a very Catholic country, very Catholic country.

speaker: interviewee
text: And while in Spain, I had it actually very good. Young girls took us-- took me and introduced me to the life there. And these young girls were actually mourning one year after a sister died. They were allowed to go out but not to dance. But I was allowed to dance and I danced. And I loved dancing. And of course, 2 and 1/2 years, it wasn't bad. It wasn't bad. And then we left, as you heard just now. And we came down, we hear all this calamity. And all of a sudden, who arrived on a lorry? My father and my brother. And that was miraculous, really. And we really counted ourselves lucky.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then it was more organized. There were lots of other lorries who took people away. And we were then taken away together with other people. And we came to Santander. In Santander, we were also assigned a house. We learned that that house belonged to a priest, who was a rightist and had run away. He didn't want to be with the populous. And we stayed in this house.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother became very, very ill. What was the matter? We don't know. And we managed to get a doctor. And the doctor said she's just exhausted. That's all. And she needs good nourishment. And we had the best nourishment that you can imagine. My mother couldn't even eat it all. We had condensed milk, sweet, wonderful condensed milk, which we hadn't-- we didn't have anything like this for a long time, any good food. And so it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every day my brother, my father, and I-- the four of us-- we would go in queues, stand in queues for bread, for whatever, you see. And so it was. The days passed. 14 days we were there. And my youngest brother, Chaim, stormed into this little house and says, there's a boat! There's a boat in the harbor. It's a coal boat! It's an English coal boat. But it's a boat, and it's taking on 2,000 refugees, and we have to queue. We packed everything, including the [NON-ENGLISH] and off we went with some food. And we stood in the queue, and we got on. And as we got on, there was a police stopped my father and my brother-- passaporte-- passports. So they didn't ask my brother, Chaim. We looked young enough, you know. And women were allowed on, of course. And they showed their passport, and they were of military age. Even if they were not Spanish, they had to stay behind. And we didn't know what was going to happen to them. And here we are on this boat, and it took us 2 and 1/2 days to get there. Well, we were very thirsty and very hungry. But the thirst-- so I took a jug, which we had with us, and I went down, right down to the thing. And there was a Scottish redhead young man. And I said, agua, por favor. I spoke Spanish now. I'm Spanish. He couldn't understand. I said, wasser! Ah, water! But I fainted going up. I fainted. I haven't got a tendency to faint. I don't know what made me faint-- what happened. I fainted and they revived me. And I was holding the jug with the water for my mother who was terribly thirsty. Eventually we arrived in Brittany, which is the West Coast of France. We arrived there. The boat docked. And you hear this funny music, this strange accordion. I mean, I knew what an accordion was, but the French are very happy people. And they're playing the accordion and it's very happy. And you know the French music, how happy it is-- the street music. But it hurt me. It hurt me. I was already used to the Spanish melancholy strains, you know, and it appealed to me. And here was this raucous music. And I couldn't-- didn't know what it meant. Anyhow, we had to get off. And I had taken my fur coat along, by the way. Oh, I wouldn't leave my fur coat behind. That's why I mentioned the fur coat before. And we came down and first food. Food! Glorious food! There was so much. The French were not at war, and there was so much. And we ate all the French-- we ate and we-- it did a lot to us. And I forgot my coat by a well. And I walked, walked, walked, and all of a  sudden, I said to my mother, my coat! But it was there. The French are honest people. It was there and I took it back. Then we had to line up. We had to line up, and here we were. We didn't know anybody, just the three of us. We had to line up. And I understood a bit of French, because I had taken five years French at the high school. So I understood a little bit. And they said, oh, cela, these ones can go to this and this town to the prison. Now it wasn't the prison-- it was a prison.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it wasn't going to be the prison for us or for the refugees. They were being looked after there, you see. But when I heard prison, I moved away. Come on, let's go. Always in Spanish. That was our language all of a sudden, you see. Come on, let's go, let's go. And we moved to another side. And there were lots of people who arranged-- how do you call it? People who-- Organized.

speaker: interviewee
text: --who helped, people who helped, who took names and said you will go here and you will go there. And I heard, oh, this group can go to [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH]. And I only want 20 of those.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there I was standing, I said, yes, ici. Ici! I'm here! And I pushed my mother. And the others also, they heard me speaking this broken French. I knew the grammar, but I couldn't speak properly. And they all pushed towards us, mainly women. And there was one boy of about 15, 16, something like that, like Chaim, you see. And they held together. And then we were herded to a bus. And we were put onto the bus.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we took a lengthy time and, we came to a small village. We were on lorry, not a bus, by the way. It was a lorry. Now I remember. We were disheveled. We looked like refugees. We were looking terrible. And there we arrived. The lorry stopped somewhere. And we hear a bell, the old fashioned bell. And people come running, looking at us. I feel like a monkey. We all felt like monkeys. Anyhow, the conference took place there with the mayor. The mayor was about 65, 70-year-old man with white, white hair. A benign fellow, a wonderful fellow. And he assured us in French that it will be all right. Don't worry. We've got a house for you, understood. And we were then taken. Oh, yes, it was announced that everybody must bring things so that people will have where to sleep, and we'll have bedding, we'll have food. Well, things started arriving into this abandoned farmhouse.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the women slept upstairs and the two boys slept downstairs. We had to eat and we had to drink. We had everything. And this mayor was wonderful. So I thought, we are here, now stuck. You know, I mean, we don't know when the war would finish. Here we are. What must I do? Oh yes, and the people, they started reveling me like I'm a goddess. I knew French! Everything came through me. And I started feeling really important. What do you mean? This 16-years-old-- by then I as 17, 17 going for 18, I think. I was the ambassadress, you see, and I arranged everything. I could speak Spanish, and now I can speak French as well. And I asked the mayor, would he let the-- oh, yes, I had some of typing skill in Germany already. But I didn't finish the course. I just knew the setting of the fingers, where to do it. But I wanted to practice in case I needed it for some work. So I asked the mayor, would he let me, in his secretary's office, just to learn typing. Oh, with pleasure. Avec plaisir, mais naturellement. So I went. Every morning I went. And I typed and I typed, all sorts of things just that my fingers should get well. One day, I think it was like the second or third week, the police arrived. They wanted to know what I'm typing. I said, practicing. So he said, what are all these funny things in this? So there was a spy. So I was not allowed to type anymore. That was my typing, and my friendship with the mayor. But the mayor was still very good to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we were there, all together three months. Now, just before the three months were up, we got a letter from my father and brother. They had managed to get somehow to Paris. And they were now in Paris. And my mother had a sister in Paris. And my father-- she helped him. He couldn't speak-- the sister or the brother-in-law helped my father find an accommodation. Now, we didn't have any money. We had pesetas, because my father had done well with his chickens. We had pesetas. But the pesetas were worthless, because there was a war, you see. So they must have helped out, my family-- I mean, my aunt and uncle must have helped him out. They rented a place in the suburbs, just out of Paris. It was called Aubervilliers. And there in Aubervilliers, he got one room. It was sort of in an enclave.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that one room was as big as this room, not bigger. And there it was. And he came, and now he says, come. And we went to the mayor. And we said we would like to go to Paris, we three you see. And the mayor says, I have to ask the police. And the police came and said you can't go. You are a Spanish refugee, you will back to Spain. So we wrote quickly to my father. They won't let us go. What must we do? My father arrived. And he spoke to a lady and explained that we are not Spanish, and we are not German, but we are Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of the sudden, our Polish nationality is very useful. We are Polish. So she conferred with somebody else, and they said fine. And we went. So we now, we were Polish, German, Spanish refugees. We were everything. And we come now to Paris. I had a little kitten, and I took it with me. I loved kittens, and I took it all the journey. The kitten was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we arrived in Paris it was by train. We went the underground to Aubervilliers, which is a little out-- the bridge, you pass the bridge, it's Aubervilliers, in the north it was, northwest. And there we arrived into this one room. What could we do? We were refugees. And you adjust very quickly to misery as well. You adjust and you make the best of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we partitioned it off-- my parents partitioned it off. And there were two cots for the brothers, a bed for me, and my parents had something there also for themselves. And it was partitioned off. This was the bedroom. This was the-- I still had my fur coat, by the way. And this was the living room. Very poor. The bare essentials. The bare essentials. Now, we have to live. The pesetas won't bring us anything. My father already was working. I don't know what he did. I forgot this. But ORT came-- O-R-T-- the society ORT, the Jewish society ORT-- came to our assistance. They took my brother Leon. Now he was called Leon all the time. They took my brother Leon, and they sent him to Hispano-Suiza factory. And in three times he knew how to make precision parts. And it is a precision work. And they paid him a beautiful salary like to everyone else.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, before that-- I'm sorry, before that he was doing something else. No, that's correct. Precision parts-- and he got a good salary. So he was looked after. They asked me what I want. Do I want to learn millinery or manicure? So I said manicure. What's the difference? Manicure-- and for six months I learned manicure. And it was all paid by ORT.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, Chaim, was very tall for his age. He was 15 or 16-- 15 at that time. He was very tall for his age. And he was emaciated. He didn't have food. We didn't have food. We didn't earn. And the ORT got him a job in a restaurant. Whether he served or whether he  just washed plates-- I think he must have washed plates, because he couldn't speak the language. By then I already had very good command of French, because I had the grammar behind me, you see. And my mother was a char. And that is how we pushed along. I remember one day, we were sitting together at supper time, and there was some meat. And I was very hungry. And my mother-- I had finished my plate-- my portion. And my mother took her meat and put it in my plate. I thought, oh, I protested. Come on, come on, she says, I'm not hungry. I don't need it, and I ate it up. And she was left with bones. I still remember, and I have a conscience about that. I haven't got a conscience about many, many things but that I have a conscience still until today. [COUGHS] And so we lived a frugal life. Now, one day my father had to go to extend his passport, his Polish passport. He didn't have the sense to get a German passport at that time. Anyhow, he comes to the-- you come into the Polish Embassy or consulate, whatever it is. You have to hand in your passport if you want it extended. You pay the fee and you wait, because there are so many hundreds of other people there to be attended to. Well, I don't know how long it took, but after a certain while the name is called out and then you take your passport, you go. Because you already have paid, you see. They make sure. And that is right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So his name was called out, Erder. He goes, takes his passport and wants to go. When all of a sudden somebody taps him on the shoulder. And he turns around. He sees a stranger. He says, are you Erder from Cologne? My father said, yes, I am. He says, well I'm Simon Puterflam. Simon Puterflam? But so many years ago? You were staying with us. Yes, he said. And how are you? And they embraced each other, and they love each other. And how is that little Santale?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that little Santale is now a very big girl. Oh, he says, I would very much like to see your wife and Santale. Please, please, won't you come to me. I'm married now. I've got a little girl. I live in Aubervilliers. But I also live in Aubervilliers. Oh, well, they rejoiced. They made arrangements that my mother and myself, we would come to their place, which was 10 minutes walk away. Just 10 minutes walk away. And my father arrived, and hear my mother shrieking with joy. Puterflam, Simon Puterflam. It was arranged that I should come along, because after all he brought the midwife. And he saw me for how many months-- eight months it must have been when he left. Of course an eight-month baby is really lovely. So I was a lovely baby and he remembered it. And so it was, we came in there. We came up and the most beautiful apartment. He was doing very well, Mr. Puterflam. He had been-- he had gone to Berlin, had met up with his brothers and had done very well. Whatever he did, I don't know, but they had done very, very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when, in 1933, Hitler had come to power, they remained for a while. But as they lived in Berlin, there was a lot of persecution. They wrote Jude on the windows. They persecuted Jews. They had already to wear the band by '35, '36. And this was 1937. They had decided to leave, and they went to-- he came as a-- what do you call it  when you have a lot of money? He came as a? Industrialist or-- entrepreneur.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not an entrepreneur. He came as a capitalist.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came as a capitalist. He had plenty of money. He even had gold ingots under his-- under his bed. And so it was that we came in. And we met this most beautiful woman. She could have been a beauty queen. Renia was her name, his wife, Renia Puterflam. And she looked at us, and she welcomed us, and we had lovely talks. It didn't take long, Renia came to visit us in our modest home. After this beautiful home, she looked at this home, but she understood. And she came very often. And we became friends, my mother and her. We became friends. We were very close. She also had come from Poland. She wasn't poor in Poland, but she also didn't have this life that she had now with her husband. And because she was so very beautiful, he had chosen her. He had gone to Poland to look for a bride and this was it, you see. And because he had all this money, she married him. And it was a happy marriage. But to come into this refugee place, she saw me washing, washing one day, because I didn't let my mother wash washing. She was frail. And it impressed her that this young girl did the washing instead of her mother. So all this, I mention from what she had in her mind. She had bees in her bonnet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Should we end it there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because that comes on--
